coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor with viscosity-regulated FRET efficiency for tracking viscosity changes in lysosome during ferroptosis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yu-Ping
Yuan, Peng-Xiang
Zhang, Jing-Yuan
Wang, Bo
Yang, Liu-Pan
Wang, Li-Li
coredata.dc:description: For various physiopathological processes, ferroptosis is a unique form of regulatory cell death and has been confirmed to be closely related to intracellular viscosity fluctuations. The dysfunction of lysosome has been considered as one of the main causes of ferroptosis. For further understanding the occurrence and development of ferroptosis, it is of great significance to reveal the viscosity fluctuations of lysosome during ferroptosis. However, it is still lack of self-calibrating ratiometric probe with high accuracy and precision for effectively tracking the viscosity changes in lysosome during ferroptosis. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on the rhodol decorated carbon dots (m-CDs-Rho) was developed to track viscosity changes in lysosome during ferroptosis. The great capacity for targeting lysosome may be ascribed to the rich amino groups on the surface of m-CDs-Rho. The m-CDs-Rho nanosensor exhibits a sensitive linear response in the viscosity range of 6.33–718 cP with the advantages of high selectivity and remarkable photostability. The enhanced Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) efficiency (increase from 5.4 % in PBS to 23.4 % in 90 % glycerol) and restricted motions of Rho are responsible for the ratiometric fluorescence response toward viscosity. Moreover, the well-separated fluorescence emission makes the developed nanosensor be suitable for high resolution bioimaging of the lysosomal viscosity. The cell imaging results suggest that m-CDs-Rho mainly distributed in lysosome, and the change of viscosity in the ferroptosis model are visualized successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ferroptosis
Lysosome-targeted
Viscosity
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Responses to viscosity in spectra measurements
The fluorescence response of m-CDs-Rho to viscosity
Response mechanism of m-CDs-Rho to viscosity
Fluorescence imaging of viscosity changes in lysosome
Fluorescence imaging of ferroptosis in cells
Ratiometric imaging of viscosity changes in zebrafish

This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174059), The Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (No. 2022RC1230), and Clinical Medical Technology Innovation Guidance Project of Hunan Province (2021SK51813). We thank USC for the technical support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface characterization of samples. TEM image, diameter distribution (the inserted figure in the top left corner), and high-resolution TEM image (the inserted figure in the top right corner) of a) m-CDs and b) m-CDs-Rho. c) XPS full survey of m-CDs and m-CDs-Rho. d) FT-IR spectra of m-CDs, Rho, and m-CDs-Rho.

Fluorescence sensing property of m-CDs-Rho. a) Fluorescence emission spectra of m-CDs-Rho (10 μg·mL−1) in the PBS-glycerol system (Insert: Images of m-CDs-Rho in 0–90 % PBS-glycerol mixed solutions under 365 nm UV lamp (n = 3). b) CIE chromaticity diagram form the fluorescence spectra of m-CDs-Rho in 0–90 % PBS-glycerol mixed solutions. c) Linear relationship between (F 640/F 505) and (η). d) Fluorescence response of m-CDs-Rho (10 μg·mL−1) to various species (100 μM). Analytes: (1) Blank; (2) K+; (3) Na+; (4) Li+; (5) Ag+; (6) Co2+; (7) Mg2+; (8) Fe2+; (9) Cu2+; (10) Al3+; (11) Fe3+; (12) Cl−; (13) NO3 −; (14) Br−; (15) I−; (16) NO2 −; (17) CH3COO−; (18) HSO3 −; (19) S2−; (20) Gly; (21) Tyr; (22) Trp; (23) Ala; (24) ClO−; (25) H2O2; (26) ascorbic acid (AA); (27) Cys; (28) GSH; (29) probe m-CDs-Rho in PBS:glycerol = 1:9 (n = 3). λex = 420 nm. slits: 5 nm, 5 nm.

Co-localization imaging of HeLa cells stained with probe m-CDs-Rho (10 μg·mL−1) (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm) and commercial dyes (500 nM) including Lyso-Tracker-Deep-Red (λex = 640 nm, λem = 662–737 nm), Mito-Tracker-Deep-Red (λex = 640 nm, λem = 662–737 nm), and Lipid-Yellow (λex = 561 nm, λem = 570–616 nm) for 30 min and fluorescence intensity correlation plots of m-CDs-Rho and commercial dyes. Scale bar = 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a) Ratiometric fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells viscosity under stimulation with ferroptosis inducer erastin (50 µM) for different time (15, 30 and 60 min) and suppressor fer-1 (50 µM) before stained with m-CDs-Rho (10 µg mL−1) for 30 min. b) The relative fluorescence intensities of the green and red channels. c) The value of F red channel/F green channel. The error bars represent ± S.D. (n = 3). Scale bar = 20 μm. λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm and 662–737 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of the nanosensor m-CDs-Rho for ratiometric response to viscosity.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor with viscosity-regulated FRET efficiency for tracking viscosity changes in lysosome during ferroptosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The First Affiliated Hospital, Institute of Anesthesiology, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001 Hunan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The First Affiliated Hospital, Institute of Anesthesiology, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, Hunan, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on the rhodamine decorated carbon dots (m-CDs-Rho) was developed to track viscosity changes in lysosome during ferroptosis for the first time. The enhanced FRET efficiency and restricted motions of Rho are responsible for the ratiometric fluorescence response toward viscosity. The fluorescence imaging results suggested that m-CDs-Rho mainly distributed in lysosome, and the change of viscosity in the ferroptosis model were visualized successfully. This ratiometric fluorescent sensor provide a reliable method to study ferroptosis and ferroptosis-related diseases.

For various physiopathological processes, ferroptosis is a unique form of regulatory cell death and has been confirmed to be closely related to intracellular viscosity fluctuations. The dysfunction of lysosome has been considered as one of the main causes of ferroptosis. For further understanding the occurrence and development of ferroptosis, it is of great significance to reveal the viscosity fluctuations of lysosome during ferroptosis. However, it is still lack of self-calibrating ratiometric probe with high accuracy and precision for effectively tracking the viscosity changes in lysosome during ferroptosis. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on the rhodol decorated carbon dots (m-CDs-Rho) was developed to track viscosity changes in lysosome during ferroptosis. The great capacity for targeting lysosome may be ascribed to the rich amino groups on the surface of m-CDs-Rho. The m-CDs-Rho nanosensor exhibits a sensitive linear response in the viscosity range of 6.33–718 cP with the advantages of high selectivity and remarkable photostability. The enhanced Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) efficiency (increase from 5.4 % in PBS to 23.4 % in 90 % glycerol) and restricted motions of Rho are responsible for the ratiometric fluorescence response toward viscosity. Moreover, the well-separated fluorescence emission makes the developed nanosensor be suitable for high resolution bioimaging of the lysosomal viscosity. The cell imaging results suggest that m-CDs-Rho mainly distributed in lysosome, and the change of viscosity in the ferroptosis model are visualized successfully.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent cell death distinct from apoptosis and autophagy [1,2]. Due to the decreased expression of the antioxidant system and the high expression of unsaturated fatty acids on the cell membrane catalyzed by excess free Fe2+, the accumulation of lipid-based reactive oxygen species results in ferroptosis [3]. Recently, ferroptosis has attracted widespread attention due to the association with a variety of diseases, including neurodegenerative diseases, T-cell immunity, cancer and so on [4,5]. Research results have suggested that the process of cellular ferroptosis is accompanied by abnormal viscosity in endoplasmic reticulum [6,7], mitochondrion [8,9], lipid droplet [10,11], lysosome [12,13] and so on. Lysosome is one of the prominent cellular storage organelles for iron and has become one of the most potential organelle targets for the investigation of ferroptosis [14]. Therefore, monitoring the lysosomal viscosity during ferroptosis and then revealing its chemobiological role in related diseases are of great significance for accurate diagnosis and preventive treatment.

Traditional viscosity measurement methods are mostly based on viscometer at macroscopic level and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) imaging at microscopic level, etc. [15,16], and hardly be applied in imaging of living cells or in vivo. Currently, fluorescence imaging technology has become a powerful method for monitoring cellular microenvironment with the superiority of simple operation, high sensitivity, non-invasive detection, and so on [17,18]. Due to these advantages, fluorescent sensors have become the major tool for viscosity measurement in vivo. Numerous fluorescent sensors have been exploited for the detection of viscosity in lysosome at present. For example, Shao's group designed and synthesized a lysosome-targeted dual-locked lysosome-targeted NIR fluorescent probe LFP-N3 for imaging H2S and viscosity [19]. Wei's group developed a wash-free and ultra-low probe IVTI for monitoring lysosomal viscosity in apoptosis [20]. However, the reported probes are based on single fluorescence emission intensity changes, which may exhibit a false result due to the complicated environmental interferences. Ratiometric probes can not only avoid environmental influences, but also achieve higher accuracy in theory [21–25]. Thus, it is of great significance to develop a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for lysosomal viscosity. To the best of our knowledge, no literature has reported the ratiometric fluorescence response to viscosity in lysosome during ferroptosis.

Rhodamine-like dyes have been widely employed in fluorescence sensors for viscosity due to their excellent optical properties [26–29]. However, most of the rhodamine-based molecules suffer from some drawbacks, such as water insolubility, poor tissue penetration (λem < 600 nm), and small Stokes shift (<30 nm) [30], which further strongly hinders their biomedical application [31]. Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in the biomedical field due to their excellent fluorescence, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, easy synthesis and modification [32,33]. Therefore, the combination of rhodamine-like dye with CDs may overcome the restriction of individual organic dye and realize the ratiometric sensing [34–36]. Herein, we develop a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for lysosomal viscosity during ferroptosis based on the rhodol dye decorated carbon dots (m-CDs-Rho) (Scheme 1 ). The FRET efficiency between donor (CDs) and acceptor (Rho) improves due to the increased absorbance in high viscosity environment and then results in the decrease of green emission and increase of red emission. Furthermore, the restricted intramolecular rotation of Rho further enhances the red fluorescence emission of m-CDs-Rho. Finally, in addition to the fluorescence ratiometric detection of viscosity values in vitro, the m-CDs-Rho nanosensor successfully achieved the ratiometric imaging of lysosomal viscosity during ferroptosis in cells for the first time.

All reagents and solvents were sourced from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd., Energy Chemical, and Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd., all based in Shanghai, China. All chemicals were purchased from commercial chemical companies and did not require further treatment and purification when used.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were acquired on Thermo Scientific Nexsa with Al Kα radiation operating at 1361 eV. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was collected on a PerkinElmer Spectrum two spectrometer in the form of postassium bromide pellets. A Nanometrics instrument was used to measure the hydrodynamic size and zeta potential. The UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra were performed on a Shimadzu spectrophotometer (UV-2600i) at 200–800 nm and a Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7100), respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes of the samples were examined using an EPL-375 spectrometer (Edinburgh). The viscosity measurement was carried out by an NDJ-8 rotational viscometer. Microplate reader for CCK-8 assay was collected from multifunctional microplate detector (Epoch, BioTek). Fluorescence imaging of cells was performed with a Nikon AX confocal microscope.

m-CDs were synthesized following the previously reported method [37]. At first, 0.04 g of m-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and sonicated for 10 min until completely dissolved. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon reactor and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. To remove unwanted solid non-fluorescent particles, the solution was purified by high-speed centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 15 min) and membrane filtration (0.22 μm) to obtain the purified carbon dots solution. Finally, the final product was dried further and collected by rotary evaporation and m-CDs were obtained as a dark brown solid.

Rhodol was synthesized following the previously reported method [38]. 2.00 g of 1,6-dihydroxynaphthalene (6.39 mmol) and 1.02 g of 2-(4-diethylamino-2-hydroxybenzoyl) benzoic acid (6.39 mmol) were dispersed in 15 mL trifluoroacetic acid, and refluxed at 80 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere for 24 h, followed by recrystallization from an ethyl acetate solution.

5.00 mg of rhodol (0.009 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL ethanol, and 38.34 mg of 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDCI) (20 mmol) and 11.51 mg of N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) (10 mmol) were added. The pH was then adjusted to 7–8 using 34.85 μL of triethylamine (20 mmol). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 5 h. Then, 12.50 mg of m-CDs were added and the reaction was continued for 8 h at room temperature. The solution was purified by centrifugation and rinsing with an ethanol/ultra-pure water mixture (1:9 v/v), and m-CDs-Rho was obtained by vacuum drying

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses to viscosity in spectra measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The solutions were prepared by mixing phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (10 mM, pH = 7.4) and glycerol at a ratio of 0–90 % by volume. The m-CDs-Rho storage solution was diluted to 10 μg·mL−1 with a mixture of PBS and glycerol in different proportions. The solutions were sonicated and mixed for 30 s, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity changes at 505 nm and 640 nm were recorded at a room temperature of 25 °C under excitation at 420 nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were both set to 5 nm.
The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was measured under 420 nm excitation using Rhodamine 6G in ethanol (Ф = 0.95) as reference.

The fluorescence lifetimes at 505 nm of m-CDs and m-CDs-Rho in PBS and 90 % glycerol were recorded under 420 nm excitation. The fluorescence lifetimes at 640 nm of Rho in PBS and 90 % glycerol were recorded under 420 nm excitation.

The FRET efficiency in PBS and 90 % glycerol were calculated by the following equation:
(1)
E = 1 -
τ
D A
/
τ D
where E is the FRET efficiency, τ D and τ DA are the fluorescence lifetime of energy donor before and after FRET respectively.

The cytotoxic effect of m-CDs-Rho was assessed by the typical procedure of commercial Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). HeLa cells in the logarithmic growth stage were seeded into 96-well plates at an appropriate density and allowed to adhere for 24 h. HeLa cells were treated with 100 µL of fresh culture medium containing m-CDs at concentrations of 0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30 µg mL−1 for 24 h. Afterward, CCK-8 was added and incubated for 1 h, and the absorbance of each well was measured at 450 nm. Finally, the relative cell survival rate was calculated based on the absorbance values using the formula: [(Asample − Ablank)/(Acontrol − Ablank)] × 100 %.

HeLa cells were seeded in confocal dishes (Nest) and allowed to adhere for 24 h in an incubator under 5 % CO2 at 37℃ before imaging. All confocal dishes were rinsed three times with PBS before confocal fluorescence imaging. The green channel (FITC, 500–550 nm) and the red channel (Cy5, 662–737 nm) were collected under 488 nm excitation, and the dual-ratio fluorescence imaging was obtained by calculating the ratio of F red to F green (F red/F green).

For colocalization imaging, HeLa cells were stained with 10 μg·mL−1 m-CDs-Rho and 500 nM Lyso-Tracker-Deep-Red, Mito-Tracker-Deep-Red, or Lipid-Yellow for 30 min. HeLa cells were stained with 10 μg·mL−1 m-CDs-Rho and 500 nM Lyso-Blue after being treated with 10 μM erastin for 30 min.

For viscosity response imaging, the control group was incubated with 10 µg mL−1 m-CDs-Rho for 30 min. The other two experimental groups were treated with 50 μM monensin or nystatin for 30 min to induce increasing cell viscosity, and then stained with 10 µg mL−1 m-CDs-Rho for another 30 min.

For viscosity monitoring imaging during ferroptosis, the control group was incubated under the same conditions as described above. In the experimental groups, HeLa cells were incubated with erastin at various concentrations (10, 20 and 50 μM) or with 50 μM erastin for different times (15, 30 and 60 min) to induce ferroptosis. The cells were then stained as described above for imaging. HeLa cells were treated with 50 μM erastin for 60 min in the presence of 50 μM Ferrostatin-1 (Fer-1) to inhibit ferroptosis.

Zebrafish were kept under optimal breeding conditions at 28 °C. Three-day-old zebrafish were transferred into dishes before imaging. All zebrafish were transferred to a new dish and washed with PBS three times before imaging.

For viscosity response imaging, the zebrafish were divided into three groups. The control group was incubated with probe (10 µg mL−1 m-CDs-Rho) for 30 min. Other two experimental groups were treated with 50 μM monensin or nystatin for 60 min respectively, and then stained with 10 µg mL−1 m-CDs-Rho for another 30 min.

To construct ratiometric nanosensor for sensing and imaging of viscosity, water-soluble CDs and Rho (Figs. S1–S3) were first synthesized according to the previous method, respectively. Then the m-CDs with abundant amino groups were modified by carboxyl Rho through amidation reaction to acquire the final fluorescence m-CDs-Rho nanosensor.

TEM images showed that the m-CDs were well-dispersed in a uniform size distribution from 1.05 to 5.26 nm with an average diameter of 2.63 nm (Fig. 1 a), while the m-CDs-Rho was dispersed in a size distribution from 1.06 to 5.44 nm with a larger average diameter of 2.92 nm (Fig. 1b). m-CDs and m-CDs-Rho all displayed clear lattice fringes with interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm (insert figure of Fig. 1a and b). The hydrodynamic sizes of m-CDs and m-CDs-Rho were 10.1 ± 0.08 nm and 12.9 ± 0.47 nm, respectively (Fig. S4a and S4b). Compared with m-CDs, m-CDs-Rho had a slight size increase and a wider size distribution due to the modification of Rho. In addition, zeta potential results showed that m-CDs was 22.6 mV and decreased to 14.4 mV with the modification of Rho (Fig. S5). The composition and chemical structure of m-CDs and m-CDs-Rho were further performed by XPS and FT-IR. The full XPS scan spectrum showed the presence of C, N, and O elements of two nanomaterials, and the N content decreased while the O content increased after the modification of Rho (Fig. 1c), which attributed to the lower N content and higher O content of Rho compared with bare m-CDs. The high-resolution C 1s scan of m-CDs (Fig. S6a) displayed three main components, including sp2 carbons (284.8 eV, C–C/C = C), sp3 carbons (285.5 eV, C–O/C–N) and C = O (288.9 eV). N 1s analysis (Fig. S6b) exhibited the presence of two peaks at 398.9 and 399.9 eV, and were attributed to the heterocyclic N–H and N–H groups, respectively. O 1s (Fig. S6c) spectrum showed three peaks at 531.3 eV (C–O), 532.3 eV (C–OH/C–O–C) and 533.0 eV (C = O). For m-CDs-Rho (Fig. S7), the position and relative content of each functional group changed compared with m-CDs, demonstrating the successful modification of Rho. Next, the disappearance of amino groups (3220/3330 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1) and the appearance of the amide groups (3680 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1) of m-CDs-Rho in FT-IR spectrum further verified the successful covalent conjugation of Rho to m-CDs (Fig. 1d).

Next, the optical properties of m-CDs, Rho and m-CDs-Rho were measured. The m-CDs displayed excellent water-solubility and showed obvious absorption between 200 nm and 500 nm in PBS solutions (Fig. S8) and the fluorescence emission of m-CDs showed a typical excitation-dependent behavior (Fig. S9). During the excitation wavelength increased from 390 to 480 nm, the maximum emission wavelength varied from 498 nm to 524 nm. The Rho dye showed maximal absorption at 555 nm in PBS buffer while strongest fluorescence emission around 640 nm (Fig. S10). The fluorescence emission of m-CDs showed well overlap with the absorption spectrum of Rho (Fig. S11), indicating the possibility of energy transfer from CDs (donor) to Rho (acceptor). As shown in Fig. S12, the as-prepared m-CDs-Rho nanosensor exhibited the characteristic absorption of CDs at 400 nm and Rho at 555 nm. The fluorescence emission of m-CDs-Rho showed dual emission peaks under single excitation, corresponding to CDs at 505 nm and Rho at 640 nm, respectively. These results further indicated that m-CDs-Rho were successfully synthesized. In addition, m-CDs-Rho also had good photostability (Fig. S13) and water-solubility (Fig. S14), which laid a foundation for it could be further used in biological processes to track viscosity changes steadily and continuously over a long period of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence response of m-CDs-Rho to viscosity
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response property of m-CDs-Rho toward viscosity were first investigated in the PBS/glycerol mixtures. As shown in Fig. S15, the m-CDs-Rho showed strong green fluorescence centred at 505 nm and weak red fluorescence centred at 640 nm in PBS buffer. However, the green fluorescence was suppressed while the red fluorescence was significantly enhanced in PBS/glycerol (1/9) mixed solutions, which displayed a ratiometric fluorescence response to viscosity. Then, the quantum yield of m-CDs-Rho was measured in PBS and 90 % glycerol solutions with 420 nm excitation (Table S1). The results show that under high viscosity conditions, the quantum yield of m-CDs in m-CDs-Rho decreased from 2.68 % to 1.56 %, while the quantum yield of Rho increased from 0.94 % to 5.21 %, indicating that the probe was sensitive to viscosity [39].

To enhance the analytical performance, the load ratio of Rho on CDs was optimized. The feeding ratios of CDs to Rho were 30:10, 25:10 and 20:10, respectively. The relative fluorescence intensity of Rho in m-CDs-Rho (F 640/F 505 value) increased as the ratio of CDs decreased (Fig. S16). With the increased viscosity, all the fluorescence ratio change tendency were similar, and the feeding ratio of 25:10 was the best due to the good linear response and obvious color change (Fig. S17). Next, the effects of different buffer types (10 mM, pH = 7.4) on m-CDs-Rho detection performance were investigated (Fig. S18). The sensor had responses to viscosity in different types of buffer solution, and the optimal response was achieved in PBS buffer. Then the response of m-CDs-Rho to viscosity at different excitation wavelength was measured to select the optimal test condition. As shown in Fig. S19, the largest response was achieved with the excitation wavelength between 400 nm to 420 nm, and the optimal excitation wavelength was 420 nm. In conclusion, 10μg mL−1 of m-CDs-Rho was fully sonicated for 30 s in a mixture of 90 % glycerol in PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4), and the response of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 640/F 505) was measured under 420 nm excitation, which were selected as the optimal experimental condition for viscosity detection.

Under the optimal conditions, the detection performance was studied carefully. The m-CDs-Rho was dissolved in various ratios of PBS/glycerol solution with fixed concentration to assess its sensitivity to varied viscosities. With the increase of glycerol content from 0 % (0.89 cP) to 90 % (718 cP), the fluorescence peak gradually quenched at 505 nm and enhanced at 640 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence color changed from green to red under 365 nm UV lamp (insert of Fig. 2a). The Commission Internationale d′Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram further showed a fluorescence color change from green to red (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, the viscosity value (η) and fluorescence intensity ratio (F 640/F 505) also showed excellent linear relationship (R2 > 0.99) in the viscosity range from 6.33 to 718 cP, with a linear equation y = 0.0016x + 0.6615 (Fig. 2c). The selectivity test results indicated that m-CDs-Rho had no significant response to other biologically relevant analytes (Fig. 2d). To verify the effect of polarity on the fluorescence of m-CDs-Rho, we measured the fluorescence in different polar solvents and the fluorescence ratio was greatly enhanced only in glycerol (Fig. S20). The fluorescence of m-CDs-Rho is mainly affected by viscosity, while the effect of solvent polarity is negligible. Meanwhile, the response of m-CDs-Rho to viscosity at different pH levels was investigated (Fig. S21). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity ratio response were relatively stable in the pH range of 4–8. Overall, the m-CDs-Rho exhibited an excellent response performance for viscosity with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity, indicating that m-CDs-Rho can detect viscosity in complex intracellular environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of m-CDs-Rho to viscosity
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the mechanism of the viscosity-induced fluorescence changes of m-CDs-Rho, controlled experiments were first carried out. The fluorescence change of individual m-CDs is irregular while the fluorescence intensity of Rho is gradually enhanced with the increased viscosity (Fig. S22). When m-CDs and Rho were mixed directly, the fluorescence response to viscosity was less sensitive compared with m-CDs-Rho nanosensor (Fig. S23). The covalent link and caused FRET between m-CDs and Rho may be responsible for the efficient sensing of viscosity. To further reveal the mechanism, fluorescence lifetimes measurements were performed. The lifetime of CDs in m-CDs-Rho nanosensor decreased from 8.18 ns to 6.63 ns with the viscosity increased from 0.89 cP to 718 cP (Fig. S24a). Moreover, the FRET efficiency increased from 5.4 % to 23.4 % according to Eq. 1. However, the lifetime of individual CDs is invariant with the changed viscosity (Fig. S24b). The significant decrease in lifetime indicates an efficient FRET between m-CDs and Rho in high viscosity solution. In addition, the absorption spectra of m-CDs-Rho was measured. As shown in Fig. S26, the characteristic absorption intensity of Rho in m-CDs-Rho nanosensor enhanced with the increased viscosity. Due to the large overlap between the emission of m-CDs and the absorption of Rho, the enhanced absorption intensity leads to the elevated FRET efficiency, which is accordance with the results of fluorescence lifetime [40,41]. The fluorescence lifetime of individual Rho prolonged with the increase of viscosity (Fig. S27), as same as other viscosity-sensitive molecular rotor probe [42–44]. Due to the enhanced FRET efficiency and restriction of rotation, the sensitive ratiometric response to viscosity was achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of viscosity changes in lysosome
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of the m-CDs-Rho nanosensor to track viscosity fluctuations of lysosome in live HeLa cells, further experiments in vivo were performed. Before cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of m-CDs-Rho was investigated by CCK-8 cell viability assay. It can be seen from the data in Fig. S28, the nanosensor showed good biocompatibility to live cells below the concentration of 30 μg·mL−1. To explore the lysosomal localization ability of m-CDs-Rho, the co-localization experiments were performed by incubating HeLa cells with m-CDs-Rho and commercial dyes including Lyso-Tracker Deep-Red, Mito-Tracker-Deep-Red and Lipid-Yellow for 30 min. Then the imaging were collected in green and red channel, respectively. The distribution of m-CDs-Rho overlapped well with Lyso-Tracker Deep-Red and the Pearson co-localization coefficient up to 0.92 (Fig. 3 ), indicating that m-CDs-Rho nanosensor exhibits great capacity for targeting the lysosome. On the contrary, the Pearson’s co-localization coefficients in mitochondria and lipid droplet were 0.67 and 0.46, respectively. The lysosome-targeting ability may be ascribed to the rich amino groups on the surface of m-CDs-Rho according to the literature report [45]. These experiment results demonstrated the high localization capability of m-CDs-Rho towards lysosome, which providing a basis for tracking the viscosity changes in lysosome during ferroptosis.

Subsequently, the m-CDs-Rho were further employed to monitor the variation of viscosity in lysosomes. Monensin and nystatin are ionophores that can disrupt intracellular ion balance, which could induce cell dehydration and viscosity increase, and were applied to change the intracellular viscosity [46–48]. First, cell imaging of Rho alone was performed under stimulation with monensin and nystatin (50 µM), and the red channel fluorescence increased (Fig. S29), which also confirming the sensitivity of Rho to viscosity. The HeLa cells showed a bright fluorescence emission in green channel along with weak emission in red channel after incubation with m-CDs-Rho (10 µg mL−1) for 30 min (Fig. S30a). By contrast, preincubation of HeLa cells with monensin and nystatin (50 µM) for 30 min and then incubation with 10 µg mL−1 m-CDs-Rho for another 30 min, the green fluorescence faded while the red fluorescence enhanced (Fig. S30a and S30b), and the relative fluorescence intensity ratio of two channels (F red/F green) increased significantly (Fig. S30c). These results were consistent with that in solution and suggesting that m-CDs-Rho could be utilized for ratiometric imaging of cell viscosity fluctuation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of ferroptosis in cells
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by its advantages of high sensitivity toward viscosity and high specificity for lysosome, drug stimulation treatment was applied to induce ferroptosis to explore whether m-CDs-Rho could respond to the change of lysosomal viscosity during HeLa intracellular ferroptosis. Erastin is a well-known ferroptosis inducer which could disrupt the cellular antioxidant defense system by blocking the uptake of extracellular Cys and inhibiting the synthesis of GSH, and then resulting in ferroptosis [49]. Prior to cell imaging, HeLa cells were divided into four groups and pretreated with 50 µM erastin for different incubation times (0, 15, 30 and 60 min) and then incubated with 10 µg mL−1 m-CDs-Rho for another 30 min. As can be seen from Fig. 4 a and b, the green fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increase of drug incubation time, while the red fluorescence intensity enhanced. When erastin was treated for 60 min, the relative intensity ratio of two channels increased 9-fold (Fig. 4c). Ferrostatin 1 (Fer-1), a ferroptosis suppressor which could effectively inhibit the considerable accumulation of lipid reactive oxygen species (ROS) [50]. When HeLa cells were treated with both 50 µM erastin and 50 µM Fer-1, there were no significant fluorescence intensity changes in the green and red channels compared with the control group (Fig. 4), indicating that the cell viscosity changes slightly when ferroptosis was inhibited. Similarly, when pretreatment with various dosages of erastin (0, 10, 20 and 50 µM) for 60 min and then with m-CDs-Rho (Fig. 31a), we also observed that the gradually decreased green fluorescence and the increased red fluorescence (Fig. 31b). Additionally, cells treated with DMSO (1 %) alone did not affect the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 31a) and the addition of single drug to m-CDs-Rho had almost no effect on the spectrum (Fig. S32), indicating that the stability of probe and the enhancement of fluorescence intensity was not caused by the drug itself. The colocalization experiments verified the m-CDs-Rho nanosensor were still located in lysosome during ferroptosis (Fig. S33). The above data shows that with the increase of the incubation time and concentration of erastin, the lysosomal viscosity continued to increase along with the degree of ferroptosis deepened, further suggesting that m-CDs-Rho could be utilized to identify dynamic changes of lysosomal viscosity during ferroptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric imaging of viscosity changes in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results of cell imaging, the imaging ability of m-CDs-Rho to viscosity changes in vivo was further explored. As exhibited in Fig. S34, the zebrafish showed strong green fluorescence and weak red fluorescence after incubated with m-CDs-Rho. As for the two groups pretreated with monensin or nystatin (50 µM) for 60 min and then incubation with m-CDs-Rho, the red fluorescence enhanced along with the decreasing of the green fluorescence. Accordingly, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio of two channels (F red/F green) increased significantly. The above results were the same as cell imaging experiments, suggesting that m-CDs-Rho could be utilized for ratiometric imaging of viscosity changes in zebrafish.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was developed to monitor the viscosity changes of lysosome during ferroptosis for the first time. The modified Rho on m-CDs employed a rotatable C–C bond as the sensitive site to viscosity changes and the ratiometric response was achieved by the synergistic effect of regulation of FRET efficiency and restricted rotation of Rho. Along with increased viscosity, the fluorescence intensity ratio value (F 640/F 505) showed a linear change. This ratiometric nanosensor m-CDs-Rho not only exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward viscosity, but also lysosome targeting capability, well-separated fluorescence emission, then achieving the monitoring of ferroptosis with high resolution and reliability in living cells under complex physiological environments. The m-CDs-Rho could be served a powerful investigation tool for ferroptosis and showed great potential application in related biomedical research.

Yu-Ping Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Peng-Xiang Yuan: Investigation, Data curation. Jing-Yuan Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Bo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Liu-Pan Yang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Li-Li Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The schematic diagram was drawn using Figdraw. This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174059), The Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (No. 2022RC1230), and Clinical Medical Technology Innovation Guidance Project of Hunan Province (2021SK51813). We thank USC for the technical support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113151.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.J. Liu, F.C. Meng, J.J. Lv, M.Y. Yang, Y.M. Wu, J. Gao, J.J. Luo, X.M. Li, G. Wei, Z.L. Yuan, H.Y. Li, Comprehensive monitoring of mitochondrial viscosity variation during different cell death processes by a NIR mitochondria-targeting fluorescence probe, Spectrochim Acta A, 295 (2023) 122602.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel fluorescent probe for visualizing viscosity changes in lipid droplets during chemotherapy-induced ferroptosis
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Wei, Y.S. Dai, J. Cao, N.Y. Fu, A novel fluorescent probe for visualizing viscosity changes in lipid droplets during chemotherapy-induced ferroptosis, Anal. Chim. Acta, 1299 (2024) 342422.
